lesson.menu_desc: Telling travel stories in the past -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Stories Worth Telling
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 13 -- Stories Worth Telling

listenings.text: Let me tell you about the most eventful day of my last trip. We had set off early for a quiet village in the mountains, expecting nothing special. While we were driving up the narrow road, the engine suddenly died, right out of the blue. So there we were, stuck, looking back at the valley below. An old farmer was passing by, and he offered to help. Eventually we ended up in his kitchen, drinking coffee, while he told us stories about the village. What looked like a disaster became the highlight of the whole journey. Sometimes the best anecdotes come from the moments that go wrong.

When I was younger, I used to travel very fast. I would visit five cities in a week and take hundreds of photos, but I could hardly recall a single moment afterward. Looking back now, I realize that I was collecting places, not stories. The real turning point came on a slow trip a few years ago. I got lost in a small town, and a stranger walked me home. That one unexpected hour stayed with me more vividly than entire fast holidays. So now I travel slowly, on purpose. I want to come home with stories worth telling, not just a longer list of stamps.

extra_audio.text: First, we set off early, before sunrise. While we were exploring the old town, we got completely lost. Then something unexpected happened: I ran into a long-lost friend. After a few mishaps, we ended up in a tiny village. Looking back, it was a real turning point and a vivid, unforgettable memory.

paras: It was meant to be an ordinary overnight train from Hanoi to the coast. I had done the trip before, and I expected to sleep through it and wake up by the sea. But somewhere around midnight, in the middle of nowhere, the train shuddered to a halt and the lights went out. For a long, strange moment, nobody moved.

Then something unexpected happened. A grandmother in the next bunk lit a small candle, opened a tin of sweets, and started telling a story to the children around her. She used to be a teacher, she said, and she still loved an audience. While she was talking, a young soldier translated for me in a whisper, and out of the blue the dark carriage felt warm and full of life.

We were stuck there for almost four hours. When the train finally jolted forward again, I was almost disappointed. Looking back, that breakdown is the only part of the journey I can still recall vividly. The sea was beautiful, of course, but it is the night the train stopped that became the story I tell at every dinner table. The best anecdotes, I have learned, rarely come from the parts that go to plan.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel essay, Slow Travel Stories

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about the train journey?
choices: The breakdown ruined an otherwise perfect trip to the sea.
The unplanned breakdown became the most memorable, story-worthy part of the trip.
Overnight trains are too unreliable to be worth taking.

items: The writer had never made this train journey before.
f
The text says 'I had done the trip before'.
The train stopped suddenly around midnight in a remote place.
t
It 'shuddered to a halt' at midnight 'in the middle of nowhere'.
The grandmother had once worked as a teacher.
t
She says she 'used to be a teacher' and still loved an audience.
The writer remembers the beach more clearly than the breakdown.
f
Only the breakdown is what the writer can 'still recall vividly'.
Why do you think the breakdown became the most memorable part of the trip, while the sea did not?
The grandmother turned a stuck train into a warm moment. What does that tell you about a good storyteller?
Think of a time when something went wrong on a trip. Looking back, did it become a story worth telling?
The writer says the best anecdotes rarely come from the parts that go to plan. Do you agree, from your own travels?

rephrase.title: Rewrite each line with the form in brackets
items: In my twenties, I often travelled alone.
used to
Every summer, my family always drove to the same beach.
would
I walked along the platform, and then the lights suddenly went out.
past continuous + when
She told stories while the children listened around her.
past continuous
We waited on the dark train, and then the engine finally restarted.
past continuous + when

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: In my twenties, I used to travel alone.
Every summer, my family would drive to the same beach.
I was walking along the platform when the lights suddenly went out.
The children were listening around her while she told stories.
We were waiting on the dark train when the engine finally restarted.
answerkey.note: Form check: used to / would + base verb for past habits; was/were + verb-ing for the longer action, interrupted by past simple after 'when'.

analyse.title: How the writer builds the story
items: Find the sentence that sets the scene at the start. What background tense does the writer use?
Find the moment the unexpected event interrupts the scene. Which words signal the sudden change?
The final paragraph gives the 'point' of the story. In your own words, what lesson does the writer draw?
